front: Work: three essays tracing (1) master vs. slave morality from ressentiment, (2) internalization of debt into guilt, (3) the meaning of ascetic ideals and the 'ascetic priest' with his 'sick sheep'
work: On the Genealogy of Morals and Master-Slave Morality

front: Philosopher: uses the image of lambs complaining about birds of prey to illustrate how morality separates strength from its expression
work: On the Genealogy of Morals and Master-Slave Morality

front: Philosopher: Foucault named his 'genealogical method' after this thinker's title method; Bernard Williams's Truth and Truthfulness also adopted it
work: On the Genealogy of Morals and Master-Slave Morality

front: Work: contrasts Apollonian (order/form) and Dionysian (chaos/ecstasy) as two artistic impulses; argues tragedy declined through Socratic rationalism
work: The Birth of Tragedy and Apollonian-Dionysian

front: Philosopher: Silenus tells Midas that the best thing for man is never to be born, illustrating Greek pessimism that tragedy overcomes
work: The Birth of Tragedy and Apollonian-Dionysian

front: Philosopher: eternal recurrence, introduced by a demon 'stealing into your loneliest existence' in The Gay Science, is called 'the most burdensome thought'
Concept: a philosopher's 'formula for greatness,' meaning to embrace anything that occurs in one's life as good or necessary; the antidote to eternal recurrence's nihilism
Work: opens with 'Suppose truth is a woman'; calls Kant 'the great Chinaman of Konigsberg'; contains the abyss aphorism and discusses 'good Europeans'
Philosopher: distinguished monumental, antiquarian, and critical approaches to historical study in the second of the Untimely Meditations ('On the Use and Abuse of History for Life')
Philosopher: declared 'God is dead' via a madman holding a lantern in The Gay Science; Ecce Homo ends the chapter 'Why I Am a Destiny' with Voltaire's 'ecrasez l'infame'
Philosopher: sister Elisabeth Forster-Nietzsche edited works to be more anti-Semitic; Montinari and Colli called The Will to Power a 'historical forgery'
Philosopher: Walter Kaufmann rehabilitated this thinker's reputation with a book calling him 'Philosopher, Psychologist, Antichrist'
Philosopher: Paul Ricoeur named this thinker one of three 'masters of suspicion' alongside Marx and Freud
Philosopher: borrowed 'Tschandala' from the Laws of Manu to contrast 'breeding' and 'taming' in religious moralities
